title: spectropath from Python
text:
spectropath is an R package. This notebook shows the simplest Python-facing workflow: call the package with Rscript, and then an optional direct bridge with rpy2.
import io
import subprocess
import pandas as pd

title: spectropath from Python
text:
Path-signature features via Rscript

R_CODE = r'''
library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

write.csv(path_features(path), stdout(), row.names = FALSE)
'''

result = subprocess.run(
    ['Rscript', '-e', R_CODE],
    check=True,
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
)

features = pd.read_csv(io.StringIO(result.stdout))
features

title: spectropath from Python
text:
Classical summaries via Rscript

R_CODE = r'''
library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

write.csv(classical_features(path), stdout(), row.names = FALSE)
'''

result = subprocess.run(
    ['Rscript', '-e', R_CODE],
    check=True,
    capture_output=True,
    text=True,
)

classical = pd.read_csv(io.StringIO(result.stdout))
classical

title: spectropath from Python
text:
Optional direct bridge with rpy2
Use this only if rpy2 is already installed in your Python environment.

import numpy as np
from rpy2.robjects import r
from rpy2.robjects import numpy2ri

numpy2ri.activate()
r('library(spectropath)')

u = np.linspace(-5, 5, 500)
f = np.exp(-0.5 * u**2) + 0.25 * np.exp(-0.5 * ((u - 1.8) / 0.45)**2)
path = np.column_stack([u, f])

r['path_features'](path)

title: Python
text:
spectropath is an R package. If your analysis stack is in Python, the cleanest option is usually to call it through Rscript and read the result back into Python.


import subprocess

code = r'''
library(spectropath)
u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)
print(path_features(path))
'''

subprocess.run(["Rscript", "-e", code], check=True)



If you already use rpy2, you can call the same functions directly from a Python session.
import numpy as np
from rpy2 import robjects

robjects.r("library(spectropath)")
robjects.r("""
u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)
print(path_features(path))
""")

title: Examples
text:
This page collects a small set of paper-aligned figures used on the site. The goal is not to reproduce every panel, but to keep a few clear visual anchors for the package.





Toy profile comparison strip.


The mock-profile experiments compare visually distinct line shapes in settings where classical summaries can become degenerate, while low-order path coordinates remain sensitive to ordered morphology.






Signed emission-absorption ordering strip.


For mixed-sign profiles, the emission–absorption ordering coordinate p_pm provides a compact way to distinguish profiles that differ mainly in the order in which emission and absorption appear along the spectral axis.






Single-galaxy IFU kinematic crop.


The IFU example shows how path-derived features can be used as a segmentation space before estimating representative velocities from stacked spectra.

title: Reference
text:
The public API is intentionally small.




path_features(path, depth = 4)
Returns the paper-facing path coordinates.


classical_features(path)
Returns classical line-profile summaries used for comparison.


as_spectral_path(u, F)
Builds a path matrix from an ordered coordinate and a measured signal.







p2
Signed velocity–flux area.


p3u
Location of asymmetry along the ordered coordinate.


p3F
Whether asymmetry is concentrated in bright or faint parts of the line.


p4F
Higher-order flux modulation.


p4T
Twist-like higher-order structure.


p_pm
Emission–absorption ordering.





library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

path_features(path)
classical_features(path)

classical_features(path)

Builds a path matrix from an ordered coordinate and a measured signal.

title: spectropath
text:
Path-signature coordinates for spectral-line morphology


Spectropath computes a compact set of low-order path-signature coordinates for spectral lines treated as ordered paths X = (u, F).
The package follows the notation used in the paper: p2, p3u, p3F, p4F, p4T, and p_pm. It is built for the cases where line shape matters: blue and red wings, shoulders, broad bases, double peaks, and mixed emission–absorption structure.

Quick start Examples Python Reference GitHub




Minimal workflow
library(spectropath)

u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

path_features(path)
      p2   p_pm    p3u    p3F    p4F    p4T
 -0.3133 0.000 -0.0169 -0.1736 0.4534 0.0132









Controlled profile comparison strip.



One of the controlled comparison strips from the paper, showing a case where a path coordinate remains discriminative in a region where width-based summaries overlap.




 


The single-object IFU example shown as galaxy context plus the starlet-masked kinematic result used in the paper workflow.








p2
Signed velocity–flux area.


p3u
Location of asymmetry along the ordered coordinate.


p3F
Whether asymmetry is concentrated in bright or faint parts of the line.


p4F
Higher-order flux modulation.


p4T
Twist-like higher-order structure.


p_pm
Emission–absorption ordering.







Install
install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("RafaelSdeSouza/spectropath")
library(spectropath)


Compute
u <- seq(-5, 5, length.out = 500)
f <- dnorm(u, 0, 1) + 0.25 * dnorm(u, 1.8, 0.45)
path <- cbind(u, f)

path_features(path)
classical_features(path)


For a larger set of paper figures and small reproducible examples, see the Examples page.
